report_name: QIZ Security Secures $17 Million in Seed Funding to Address Post-Quantum Cryptographic Risks

vulnerabilities.name: Post-Quantum Cryptographic Weakness
vulnerabilities.description: Current public-key cryptography algorithms, such as RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), are vulnerable to being broken by a sufficiently powerful quantum computer running Shor's algorithm. This enables 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks on currently encrypted data.

campaigns.name: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later
campaigns.description: A type of campaign where malicious actors exfiltrate large volumes of encrypted data with the intention of decrypting it in the future once a powerful quantum computer becomes available.
campaigns.objective: Steal encrypted data for future decryption

identities.name: QIZ Security
identities.description: A cybersecurity startup specializing in post-quantum cryptographic (PQC) risk management that raised $17 million in seed funding to develop its platform.

identities.name: Bessemer Venture Partners
identities.description: A venture capital firm that co-led the $17 million seed funding round for QIZ Security.

identities.name: Merlin Ventures
identities.description: A venture capital firm that co-led the $17 million seed funding round for QIZ Security.

identities.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
identities.description: The U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) is in the final stages of standardizing a suite of PQC algorithms to defend against quantum threats.

identities.name: National Security Agency
identities.description: The U.S. National Security Agency (NSA) has released its Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0 (CNSA 2.0), which mandates timelines for transitioning to quantum-resistant algorithms for national security systems.

relationships.source_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
National Institute of Standards and Technology
